title: Linked List Cycle
description:
A singly linked list is described by a plain list of values, `values`, in head-to-tail order, plus an integer `pos`: the index (0-based) in `values` that the LAST node's `next` pointer is rewired to, forming a cycle back into the list. If `pos` is `-1`, the list is a normal, cycle-free chain. Determine whether the list (after wiring the cycle) contains a cycle.

**Example**

```
values = [3, 2, 0, -4], pos = 1   (tail's next points back to index 1, value 2)
has_cycle(values, pos)  ->  True

values = [1, 2], pos = -1        (no cycle)
has_cycle(values, pos)  ->  False
```
starter_code:
def has_cycle(values, pos):
    # values: list of node values head-to-tail.
    # pos: index the tail's next points to, or -1 for no cycle.
    # Return True if the list has a cycle, else False.
    pass
hints: Build the actual nodes first, then wire the last node's next to the node at index pos (skip wiring if pos is -1 or the list is empty) so you have a real, possibly-cyclic linked list to traverse.
Use Floyd's tortoise-and-hare: a slow pointer moving one step and a fast pointer moving two steps at a time. If they ever meet, there's a cycle; if fast falls off the end (hits None), there isn't.

solutions.name: Build nodes + wire cycle, then fast/slow pointers
solutions.explanation: Build real nodes for values and link them head-to-tail, keeping a list of node references so we can wire the cycle. If pos != -1 and the list is non-empty, set the last node's next to the node at index pos. Then run Floyd's cycle detection: slow advances one step, fast advances two steps per iteration; if they ever become the same node, a cycle exists. If fast (or fast.next) reaches None, there is no cycle.
solutions.complexity: Time O(n), Space O(1) (excluding the O(n) list construction)
